title: Small Modern Chicken Coop
summary: A slant-roof coop that doesn't look agricultural: cedar picket siding, an off-the-shelf tin roof panel, removable floor, and doors sized for actual cleaning access.
description:
Most backyard coops look like a shed had a rummage sale. This one is deliberately modern — a single 5° slant roof of off-the-shelf tin, clean cedar picket siding, and proportions that survive being visible from the kitchen window. Underneath the looks it's a thoroughly practical two-level coop: wire-wrapped run below, sided roost above, nesting box from plywood scraps.

The plan is full of the builder's own field corrections, which are the best parts. The floor is cut 1/2" small and NOT screwed down, so it lifts out for cleaning, with a lip added at the back to keep bedding from avalanching when the door opens. The back door was upgraded to full-height after living with a smaller one, because cleaning access is the difference between a coop you maintain and one you resent. And the paint tip is underlined for a reason: finish the frame interiors BEFORE stapling on the chicken wire — painting through mesh is misery.

The picket siding uses a smart taper method: cut the first picket at 5° to match the roof slope, then use each piece's short point to measure the next, working down the slope and eventually cutting from offcuts. Use hardware cloth rather than chicken wire if raccoons are serious in your area — wire keeps chickens in; cloth keeps predators out.

tools.note: The frames are 1-1/2" pocket holes with 2-1/2" exterior pocket screws throughout.
The repeated 5° cuts for frame tops and siding.
Chicken wire or hardware cloth. Fold cut edges over before stapling — the trimmed ends are needle-sharp.
The plywood floor and nesting box parts.

materials.name: 2x4, 10 ft
materials.note: The starred long cuts come from these — cut the longest pieces first.

materials.name: Cedar fence picket, 5-1/2" x 72"
materials.note: All the siding. (1/2" exterior plywood substitutes, but the source warns: two full sheets and a lot of waste.)

materials.name: Tin roof panel, 37-1/2" x 8 ft
materials.note: The design is dimensioned around this stock sheet — buy it first and confirm the size.

materials.name: Chicken wire or hardware cloth, 36" wide
materials.note: Hardware cloth costs more and is worth it — raccoons open chicken wire.

materials.name: Chicken wire or hardware cloth, 24" wide
Exterior hinges
materials.note: Two sets — front and back doors.

cutList.note: Both ends 5° off square, parallel, long point to short point — the roof slope.
One end 5° off square, long point measurement. From the 10-footers.
One end 5°, long point. From the 10-footers.
One end 5°, long point. From the 10-footers.
Then trimmed ~1/2" small to lift out — see the steps.
Reduced from the drawn 56-3/8" for swing clearance. From the 10-footers.

steps.title: Build the two side frames — in mirror
steps.body:
Lay out the first side frame (sloped top rail, three graduated uprights, bottom and mid rails), mark and drill the 1-1/2" pocket holes, and assemble with 2-1/2" exterior pocket screws. Build the second frame IN MIRROR — the frames face each other.

Do the builder's regret-tip now: paint or seal the frame interiors before any wire goes on. Painting through stapled mesh is the worst hour of the whole project.

steps.title: Wire the side frames
steps.body: Staple chicken wire or hardware cloth to the frame insides. Trim the excess and FOLD the cut edges over before the final staples — trimmed wire ends are needle-sharp, and both you and the birds will find them otherwise.
materials: Chicken wire or hardware cloth, 36" wide

steps.title: Add the floor cleats
steps.body: Screw the 2x2 floor cleats to the frame insides with the 2-1/2" screws — these carry the removable roost floor.

steps.title: Start the siding — the taper method
steps.body: Cut the first cedar picket with its top at 5° (long-point measurement) and attach to the side wall inside face, flush with the 2x2 top, leaving at least 1-1/2" of frame exposed above. Two of the shorter screws per joint.

steps.title: Work down the slope
steps.body: Measure the SHORT point of the picket you just installed — that's the LONG point of the next one. Cut, install, repeat down the slope. The first few cuts need fresh pickets; before long the offcuts are supplying the pieces, which is the method's whole economy.

steps.title: Join the sides with the cross members
steps.body:
Drill two 1-1/2" pocket holes in each end of the 33" 2x4s and attach them to one side frame, then flip the assembly onto the second frame and attach. The coop becomes three-dimensional here — a second pair of hands saves a lot of awkward propping.

(The source left one bottom-end 2x4 off for easier cleaning access — shown in photos, optional.)

steps.title: Trim and side the interior wall
steps.body: Add the 2x2 interior trim, then side the interior wall with pickets and the shorter screws, saving offcuts for the doors. The upper opening this creates is both ventilation and the birds' door to the run — it stays open because the run below is enclosed.

steps.title: Fit the removable floor
steps.body: Cut the plywood floor to fit its opening MINUS about 1/2" all around, set it on the cleats, and do not screw it down. A floor you can lift out is a coop you can actually clean. Add a lip strip at the back edge — the source's own improvement — so bedding doesn't pour out when the door opens.

steps.title: Build and hang the front door
steps.body: Pocket-hole the front door frame together, staple wire to its back, and hang it with hinges and a latch. Check the swing clears the siding before final hinge screws.
materials: Chicken wire or hardware cloth, 24" wide

steps.title: Build and hang the full back door
steps.body: Build the back door — pickets over the upper portion, wire over the lower — and hang it. This full-height door is the source's post-build upgrade: it makes the lower level cleanable without crawling, which is the feature you'll thank yourself for monthly.
materials: Chicken wire or hardware cloth, 24" wide

steps.title: Roof it with the tin panel
steps.body: Slide the 37-1/2" x 8 ft tin sheet onto the sloped frame tops and fasten with the metal roofing screws into the 2x4s. The whole coop was dimensioned around this off-the-shelf panel — no cutting, no seams, no leaks to create.

steps.title: Build the nesting box
steps.body: Assemble the nesting box from the plywood pieces — floor, three dividers, 1x3 lip — with pocket holes or 2-1/2" screws, and install it in the roost. Bed it, introduce the flock, and accept that the coop now looks better than the shed.

images.alt: Small modern chicken coop with slanted tin roof and cedar picket siding
